answers.intake_q1: When did your elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: What activities or injuries, if any, preceded the onset of your elbow pain?
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your hand or arm?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your elbow pain, and did they help?
answers.intake_a6: Icing, but only the first day. It helped at little.
answers.intake_q7: Any pain or difficulty when you try to grip or lift objects with your hand?
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other signs of infection since the pain started?
answers.intake_q9: Any prior history of elbow problems or other joint issues?
answers.intake_summary: Patient has 3 weeks of lateral elbow pain after playing tennis, no swelling, redness, or neurologic symptoms, mild grip difficulty, and minimal relief from initial icing. No prior joint issues or signs of infection.

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow bone. The pain began after playing tennis and is described as mild but persistent. She reports no swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, or weakness in the hand or arm. She has experienced slight difficulty gripping or lifting objects. Initial treatment with icing on the first day provided minimal relief. There are no systemic symptoms such as fever or chills, and no prior history of elbow or joint problems. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain following repetitive wrist extension activities such as tennis. The absence of swelling, redness, or neurologic symptoms supports this diagnosis.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen for pain control if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; ensure patient is aware of dosing limits and potential side effects. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if pain worsens, if there is new swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, or if systemic symptoms such as fever develop. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time given the typical presentation; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist beyond 6-8 weeks or worsen despite conservative management.

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. evidence.title: Tendinitis: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: onnect muscles to bones. They help your muscles move your bones. Tendinitis is the severe swelling of a tendon. Tendinitis usually happens after repeated injury to an area such as the wrist or ankle. It causes pain and soreness around a joint. Some common forms of tendinitis are named after the sports that increase their risk. They include tennis elbow, golf…

evidence.snippet: s Summary Most kinds of arthritis cause pain and swelling in your joints. Joints are places where two bones meet, such as your elbow or knee. Infectious arthritis is an infection in the joint. The infection comes from a bacterial, viral, or fungal infection that spreads from another part of the body.
